_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: Current commodity prices show WTI crude at $71.87 per barrel and Brent crude at $71.59 per barrel, with US regular gasoline priced at $3.83 per gallon as of June 2026. Henry Hub natural gas stands at $3.33 per MMBtu and is noted as the most volatile of these fuels, evidenced by its sharp spike in 2022 before retreating, when indexed to 2019 levels set at 100. Crude oil and gasoline prices have exhibited more synchronized movements compared to the fluctuating natural gas market, reflecting differing demand and supply dynamics.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity, which measures the average cost per megawatt-hour ($/MWh) over a project's lifetime, shows onshore wind at $33/MWh and solar PV at $44/MWh, both significantly cheaper than coal at $110/MWh and gas combined cycle gas turbines (CCGT) at $76/MWh. Offshore wind costs $75/MWh, also competitive yet higher than onshore options. Solar PV’s approximately 90% cost decline since 2010, from around $460/MWh to $44/MWh, underscores substantial technological and efficiency gains that now make renewables more cost-effective than new fossil fuel-based generation.
notes.currency: An estimated 20% of global oil trade is now settled outside the US dollar, marking a gradual shift though the exact figure is unofficial. Despite this trend, the US dollar continues to dominate oil transactions globally. The rise in non-dollar settlements is primarily driven by Russia’s pivot towards Asian buyers along with increased use of the yuan and rupee for transaction settlements, reflecting regional currency diversification but not supplanting the dollar’s main role.
notes.now: Today’s energy landscape is shaped by moderately priced crude and gasoline, a historically volatile natural gas market, and a rapid decline in renewable generation costs making clean energy increasingly competitive. Concurrently, financial aspects of oil trading show a modest diversification away from the US dollar without displacing its dominance. Together, these elements highlight ongoing shifts in fuel economics, technology, and international trade settlements without indicating immediate market upheaval.
